title: Article 19
lead: The longest set of rights and restrictions, heavily derived by the courts with several US and UK tests. Despite its broad coverage, Art 19 evolved chiefly as a ==pro-democracy, press, dissent and speech right==.
intro: “Freedom of opinion and freedom of association are the two lungs that are absolutely necessary for a man to breathe the oxygen of liberty.” — Gandhi, 36th Congress Session (1921)

items.label: India's rank, World Press Freedom Index 2024/25
freedoms under Art 19(1): speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, profession
grounds of restriction on speech under Art 19(2)

blocks.text: ==Restrictions (Art 19(2))==: sovereignty & integrity · security · friendly foreign relations · public order · decency/morality · contempt of court · defamation · incitement to an offence.

items.title: Speech (Dissent)
bullets: ==Protest==: ‘Heckler's Veto’ US test (1966); [[Re: Ramlila Maidan (2012)]] — implied FR; [[Amit Sahni v. Delhi Police (2020)]] (Shaheen Bagh) — right upheld but ==no right to protest indefinitely==
==Strike==: [[T.K. Rangarajan v. TN (2003)]] — not a fundamental or statutory right
==Hate speech==: not specifically defined — s.196 BNS
==Sedition==: BNS drops the term but ==enhances its scope==

items.title: Expression (Censorship)
bullets: ==Press==: [[Romesh Thappar (1950)]] freedom to publish · [[Sakal Papers (1962)]] freedom to propagate · [[Bennett Coleman (1972)]] qualitative and quantitative
==Print==: [[Tata Press v. MTNL (1995)]] — includes ==commercial speech==
==Films==: [[Ranjit D. Udeshi (1964)]] narrow UK ==Hicklin test== · [[K.A. Abbas (1971)]] films treated separately · [[Aveek Sarkar (2014)]] US Roth ==community standards test==
==Broadcasting==: [[MIB v. Cricket Assn of WB (1995)]] — airwaves are public property
==Digital==: [[Shreya Singhal (2015)]] — struck down ==s.66A IT Act==

items.title: Derived Rights
bullets: ==Right to know / RTI==: [[State of UP v. Raj Narain (1975)]]
==Right to silence==: [[Re: Noise Pollution (2005)]]; [[Bijoe Emmanuel (1986)]] (national anthem)
==Flying the national flag==: [[UoI v. Naveen Jindal (2004)]]
==Travel abroad==: [[Maneka Gandhi (1978)]]
==Against arbitrary internet shutdowns==: [[Anuradha Bhasin (2020)]]
==Against non-state actors==: [[Kaushal Kishore v. UP (2023)]]

items.title: Restrictions Jurisprudence
bullets: [[State of Madras v. V.G. Row (1952)]]: test of ==specific reasonableness==
==Phone tapping==: [[PUCL v. UoI (1997)]]
==Contempt==: Contempt of Courts Act, 1971
==Defamation==: [[Subramanian Swamy (2016)]] upheld criminalisation
==Privileges==: Searchlight Case — MP/MLA FoE > citizen FoE

blocks.text: ==Contemporary developments==: Ashoka University professor's post on Op. Sindoor · Wikimedia Foundation case · India's Got Latent (vulgarity/obscenity) · Vikatan cartoon · SC quashed case against an MP for a poem · Bombay HC struck down the ==Fact Check Unit== (IT Rules) · Samsung TN workers' right to unionise · US revoking overseas student visas.

head: Freedom
Restrictions
Key jurisprudence
rows: ==Peaceful assembly without arms== — 19(1)(b)
Sovereignty & integrity; public order

[[Babulal Parate (1961)]] processions subject to Magistrate's directions · [[Himat Lal K. Shah (1973)]] may regulate, ==cannot prohibit all== · [[MKSS v. UoI (2018)]] right to peaceful protest · [[Natasha Narwal (2023)]] protest ≠ terror act under UAPA · Police Act 1861, ==s.163 BNSS (old s.144 CrPC)==, AFSPA 1958
==Associations, unions, co-ops== — 19(1)(c)
Sovereignty & integrity; public order; morality
[[Tika Ramji (1955)]] right ==not to join== · [[Zoroastrian Coop Housing (2005)]] no FR to be a member · [[Raghubir Dayal (1968)]] no right to fulfil objectives
==Movement== — 19(1)(d) & ==Reside/settle== — 19(1)(e)
General public interest; ==protection of STs==

[[AK Gopalan (1950)]] no effect on preventive detention · [[Kaushailiya (1963)]] sex workers' movement restrictable · [[Kharak Singh (1963)]] psychological inhibition counts · [[Rajneesh Kapoor (2007)]] helmet rule is no restriction · ILP, Epidemic Diseases Act, DM Act, Goonda Acts
==Profession, trade, business== — 19(1)(g)
General public interest; qualifications; nationalisation

[[RMD Chamarbaugwalla (1957)]] ==crime cannot be a business== · [[Sodan Singh (1989)]] hawkers subject to laws · [[Khoday Distilleries (1995)]] no right to trade in liquor (Art 47 DPSP) · [[TMA Pai (2002)]] education is charitable occupation

blocks.text: India is the ==5th most digitised country== (State of India's Digital Economy 2026) — ==2nd largest mobile connections (1.14 bn)==, 2nd largest 5G market. The ==IT Act, 2000 + IT Rules, 2021== form the legal architecture for online content.

items.title: S.69A — Blocking of Online Content
bullets: Centre can direct intermediaries to ==block public access== on sovereignty, security, foreign relations, public order grounds
Upheld in [[Shreya Singhal (2015)]] ==only with safeguards== — reasoned order, hearing, review committee (Blocking Rules 2009)

Issues: ==secrecy (Rule 16)== blocks scrutiny (Tanul Thakur 2022; X Corp's 2025 ==Sahyog portal== challenge); no post-decisional hearing; vague grounds; executive-only review; ==chilling effect==; emergency blocking as routine

items.title: S.79(3)(b) — Safe Harbour
bullets: Conditional immunity for intermediaries as ==passive conduits== with due diligence; lost on failing to act on ==‘actual knowledge’==
Shreya Singhal: actual knowledge = ==court/government order== — yet informal takedown pressure persists

Issues: ==‘expeditiously’ undefined==; privatised censorship (over-removal); ==traceability (Rule 4(2)) vs end-to-end encryption== (WhatsApp's Delhi HC challenge); no judicial-determination requirement; intermediaries as quasi-judges

items.title: IT Rules, 2021 — Broader Issues
bullets: Due diligence + ==36-hour takedown== + three-tier grievance mechanism + Code of Ethics for digital news/OTT; extra duties for SSMIs
==Fact Check Unit== makes govt sole arbiter of ‘fake’ content about itself — challenged in [[Kunal Kamra v. UoI]]
==Ultra vires concerns==: subordinate rules imposing substantive speech restrictions; executive as final appellate authority over digital news
Traceability vs [[Puttaswamy (2017)]] privacy; overlaps with DPDP Act 2023; ad-hoc amendments (deepfake/AI-labelling 2025) breed unpredictability

items.title: Way Forward
bullets: A principle-based ==Digital India Act== replacing the 2000-era IT Act, with narrow definitions and judicial safeguards
Mandatory ==reasoned orders + post-decisional hearing + independent review== under s.69A
Codify ‘actual knowledge’ and timelines under 79(3)(b)
==Independent multi-stakeholder fact-checking== body; risk-based graded due diligence; ==protect end-to-end encryption==; institutionalise pre-legislative consultation

head: Hate speech
Sedition
rows: Speech demeaning/inciting discrimination or violence against a ==person/group== on religion, race, caste, sex etc.
Speech/acts inciting hatred or ==disaffection against the State==, encouraging secession/armed rebellion.
Target: ==dignity & equality of groups== — citizen vs citizen (horizontal).
Target: ==sovereignty, unity & security== — citizen vs State (vertical).
No standalone definition — pieced from ==BNS ss.196, 197, 299==.
Word ‘sedition’ dropped from BNS 2023 — ==substantively replaced & widened by s.152==.
Art 19(2) grounds: public order, decency/morality, incitement.
Art 19(2) grounds: security of the State, sovereignty & integrity, public order.

head: Law
Provision
Gist
rows: Acts endangering sovereignty/unity — secession, armed rebellion, subversive activities; ==life or up to 7 yrs== (replaces s.124A IPC; widens to electronic/financial means).
Promoting enmity between groups (up to 3 yrs); imputations against national integration (old s.153A/153B IPC).
Deliberate malicious acts wounding religious feelings — up to 3 yrs (old s.295A/298 IPC).
IT Act, 2000
s.66A (struck), 69A, 79
s.66A struck down (2015); s.69A blocking power; s.79 conditional safe harbour.
IT Rules, 2021
Rule 3
Due diligence & 24/36-hr takedown duty for unlawful/hateful content.
RPA 1951 / UAPA 1967
Election disqualification for communal enmity; UAPA 'unlawful activities' — often clubbed with BNS s.152.

head: Case
Year
Holding
rows: [[Kedar Nath Singh v. State of Bihar]]
Upheld s.124A but narrowed: only ==incitement to violence== or tendency to public disorder is seditious — mere criticism is not.
[[S. Rangarajan v. P. Jagjivan Ram]]
Restriction needs ==proximate/direct nexus== to public disorder, not speculative apprehension.
[[Balwant Singh v. State of Punjab]]
Isolated sloganeering without incitement ==is not sedition==.
[[Shreya Singhal v. UoI]]
Struck down s.66A as ==vague & overbroad==; upheld s.69A with safeguards; drew the ==advocacy vs incitement== line.
[[Patricia Mukhim v. State of Meghalaya]]
Criticism of ethnic favouritism ==without intent to incite== is protected speech.
[[S.G. Vombatkere v. UoI]]
==s.124A kept in abeyance== — fresh sedition prosecutions halted until BNS replaced it.

items.label: hate-speech cases (old s.153A) registered in 2020 (NCRB); conviction rate <20%
sedition cases by 2019-20, up from single digits pre-2014; conviction ~3%
Press Freedom Index 2024/25 — speech cases against journalists cited

items.title: Issues
bullets: No standalone, precise definition of hate speech — vague terms (‘disaffection’, ‘enmity’) ==enable misuse against dissent==
==Low conviction, high registration== → chilling effect
Digital amplification (social media, deepfakes) outpaces enforcement

items.title: Way Forward
bullets: ==Law Commission 267th Report==: codify a narrow hate-speech provision; embed the ==Kedar Nath incitement test== statutorily
Prior screening before FIR in speech offences; police sensitisation
AI-based detection, faster intermediary accountability, periodic IT Rules review

blocks.text: The true test of a democracy is not in how it grants rights, but in how it restrains power. The balance must not be mechanical, but moral and constitutional — one that ensures liberty does not descend into license, nor order into oppression. Only then can Article 19 be what it was always meant to be: the lifeblood of Indian democracy.
